data.title: Unibail-Rodamco-Westfield May Become A High-Dividend Yielder Over The Next Couple Of Years (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
As I’ve covered in previous articles, Unibail-Rodamco-Westfield (UNIRF) was one of the European real estate companies most affected by the pandemic due to its focus on retail assets, but its business has recovered quite rapidly over the past couple of years and is now on a much stronger position.

Indeed, since my last article on Unibail, the company has released its 2024 earnings, which maintained a positive operating momentum and the company was able to further improve its balance sheet, being now in a stronger financial position than compared to some years ago.

Its net rental income increased in all business segments, showing that momentum was positive across the group. Shopping centres reported an increase of 5.8% like-for-line (LfL) on net rental income to €2.07 billion during 2024, offices reported an increase of 14.4% (LfL) to €102 million, and convention & exhibition was up by 21% (LfL) to €139 million due to the Paris Olympics and Paralympics. Overall, its net rental income amounted to €2.3 billion, up by 6.7% LfL, but increased only by 4.7% on a reported basis due to asset sales.

While Unibail has largely completed its asset disposal program initiated in 2021, the company maintains its asset rotation strategy being always looking for ways to have the best assets and dispose of the ones that no longer make sense to hold in the investment portfolio. Given this background, Unibail made around €1.6 billion of asset disposals during 2024, while invested around €600 million to buy the remaining stakes in three assets that it didn’t fully own.

Its disposals were around €1 billion related to retail assets and €500 million related to offices, being performed at book value. This means these sales didn’t generate losses or gains in the company’s P&L account but raised about €1 billion of cash that was used to reduce its indebtedness.

Beyond asset sales and purchases, the company also completed some projects during the last year, both in the retail and office segments. These projects represented a total investment cost of about €300 million and were on average let at 88%, leading to a yield on cost of about 6%.

Following these asset changes, the gross market value of investment properties amounted to €49.7 billion at the end of 2024, representing an increase of 0.3% YoY. However, investors should note that on a net basis, Unibail performed more disposals than acquisitions, which means there was a positive revaluation effect for the remaining part of its portfolio during 2024.

This is a positive development for the strength of its balance sheet, as the loan-to-value [LTV] ratio – a key measure of leverage in the European real estate sector – declines due to higher asset values. As shown in the next graph, its LTV ratio benefited both from higher asset values and a lower net debt level (including signed disposals that weren’t completed at year-end), leading to an LTV ratio of 44.7% at the end of 2024.

This means its LTV ratio declined by 80 basis points (bps) during the last year and is now below 45%, which is usually the threshold credit rating agencies require maintaining an investment grade credit rating. Therefore, following several years of reporting a higher than desired LTV ratio, that resulted on a lot of pressure on Unibail’s management to protect the balance sheet, Unibail is now in a much stronger position and has much less pressure to perform asset sales ahead.

Moreover, its cash flows were also pressured in the past from its relatively large investment pipeline, as the company had commitments that it couldn’t withdraw from. The most important one was its large project in Hamburg, which initially was expected to cost about 1.6 billion. Due to some unexpected issues, this project had higher construction costs, leading to a total investment of €2.16 billion, being a drag on its cash flow generation in the recent past. This project was completed last April and opened to the public, being 95% let at the time.

This background is also supportive for the company’s balance sheet as capital expenditures should decline considerably after 2025, leading to higher cash flow generation and improved ability to organically reduce its indebtedness.

Indeed, the company expects to maintain a declining LTV ratio over the next couple of years, targeting a LTV ratio around 41.6% including hybrid debt by the end of 2026, with the vast majority of reduction coming from earnings. Nevertheless, Unibail still expects to make asset disposals during this period, while capex and dividend distributions will be the major headwinds for a lower LTV ratio ahead.

This means that Unibail is still somewhat focused on improving its balance sheet in the short to medium term, wanting to have some buffer against a potential market downturn in the future. I think this approach makes sense and shows that Unibail’s management is clearly concerned about the sustainability of the business model. On the other hand, this also means capital returns are not expected to become a priority over the next couple of years, even though Unibail is also committed to delivering an attractive shareholder remuneration policy in the future.

Related to 2024 earnings, the company decided to increase its dividend payment to €3.50 per share, representing an annual increase of about 40%. This strong increase was justified mainly by the dividend’s relatively low base, rather than earnings growth. Indeed, its adjusted recurring EPS was up by only 2.1% YoY to €9.85 per share, showing that dividend growth was largely justified by a higher payout ratio. Indeed, the payout ratio increased from 26% related to 2023 earnings to 36% in 2024, being the major driver of dividend growth.

During the first quarter of 2025, Unibail only reported a trading update as like many other European companies it only reports full earnings twice per year. In Q1 2025, its total revenues amounted to €800 million, up by 0.2% YoY, as growth in shopping centres was offset by a cyclical decline in events & exhibition. Unibail also completed and secured about €1 billion of asset disposals since the beginning of 2025, being the major driver for the company to report slower revenue growth this year and a drop on recurring EPS to a level between €9.30-9.50, representing an annual decline between 3.5% to 5.6%.

Despite lower earnings, Unibail has presented new financial targets for the period 2025-28, aiming to reach mid-single digit earnings growth and higher shareholder payouts ahead. For 2025, its guidance is to increase the dividend to €4.50 per share (+29% YoY), representing a dividend payout ratio of about 47%, while it expects to increase the payout to 60% in 2026, and achieve a normalized payout between 60-70% by 2027. Therefore, even if earnings growth over the next few years are not expected to be impressive, Unibail’s dividend growth prospects are strong due to a higher payout expected over the coming three years.

Assuming a dividend payout ratio of about 65% by 2027, its dividend can increase to about €6.45 per share in that year, which at its current share price leads to a forward dividend yield of about 8%. In the short term, its dividend guidance related to 2025 earnings leads to a forward dividend yield of about 5.6%, showing that Unibail is likely to become again a high-dividend yielder over the coming quarters, being a supportive factor for a higher share price ahead.

Regarding its valuation, Unibail continues to trade at a significant discount to Net Tangible Assets [NTA] per share, given that NTA was €116.9 per share at the end of 2024. This means Unibail is currently trading at around 0.69x NTA, which is a slightly lower valuation compared to when I last analysed the company (it was trading at 0.74x NTA at the time). I see this valuation as undemanding, especially now that Unibail has set a clear path to deliver a growing dividend over the next few years, which is a positive factor for its investment case going forward.

Conclusion

While Unibail’s growth prospects aren’t impressive ahead, the company is now on a much stronger financial position and has a clear path to reach a very acceptable LTV ratio below 42% by 2026, allowing it to become more aggressive toward dividend distributions over the next few years. If Unibail maintains the good execution of its business plan over the next couple of years, it has good prospects of becoming again a high-dividend yielder. Furthermore, its valuation continues to be relatively undemanding, making it quite attractive for income-oriented investors within the European real estate sector.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Trade Desk: The Easy Money Has Been Made, But Why I'm Staying Long
data.description:
The Trade Desk (TTD) has been a big winner as management returned to their “beat and raise” ways. The company continues to grow both its top and bottom lines very rapidly even amidst macro headwinds. TTD is still addressing a large advertising opportunity which may allow it to sustain aggressive revenue growth rates for many years to come. The company maintains a solid net cash balance sheet and is profitable on a GAAP basis. I no longer see a case for immediate multiple expansion but still see excess returns over the long term due to the growth opportunity. I rate the stock a buy rating.

TTD Stock Price

I last covered TTD in April where I upgraded the stock on account of the cheap valuation. The stock has soared over 40% since then.

This latest rally was quite abrupt and sudden, and I suspect it was mainly due to low expectations, a phenomenon that has since been addressed.

TTD Stock Key Metrics

TTD has been one of the big beneficiaries of the shift to online advertising, as it is one of the largest independent demand-side platforms (‘DSPs’) which means that it helps advertisers place advertisements across the internet.

The latest quarter saw the company deliver 25.4% YoY revenue growth to $616 million, crushing guidance of $575 million.

Adjusted EBITDA of $208 million also demolished guidance of $145 million. To anybody new to the story, these results might not seem that impressive in its own right, but the stock had fallen precipitously in the prior report, apparently due to the first quarter guidance. I am of the view that the stock’s huge rise since is indicative of management succeeding in right-sizing expectations.

TTD ended the quarter with $1.7 billion of cash versus no debt, representing a strong balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management has guided for the company to see 16.6% YoY revenue growth to $682 million and adjusted EBITDA of $259 million. While this represents a steep deceleration from first quarter numbers, it looks like the market is expecting the company to return to the “beat and raise” habits that made the stock famous.

On the conference call, management credited their strong results as being due to them taking market share. Management also framed their company as being a likely beneficiary from the ongoing anti-trust case against Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), noting that they have “been winning in an unfair market” and “are even more confident we can win in a fair market.”

Is TTD Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

After the surge from the lows, TTD is now trading just under 40x earnings.

Consensus estimates call for the company to sustain aggressive top-line growth for as far as the eye can see.

TTD is back to enjoying premium valuations - the 38x P/E ratio and the 12x P/S ratio are a premium to the 18x to 28x ratios and 5x to 10x ratios seen at GOOGL and Meta Platforms (META), respectively. Bulls might say that this is justified by the longer growth runway, but as just mentioned, consensus estimates are already reflecting this bullishness. I do not see an immediate reason for the stock to soar here - the easy money has been made. However, the long growth runway might allow for continued alpha to be had. I again assume that TTD can achieve 35% net margins over the long term. I can see the stock trading at around 10x sales in 2033, reflecting a 29x earnings multiple. That suggests around 15% potential annual returns through 2033 (or around 16% inclusive of the earnings yield), assuming TTD can hit consensus growth estimates. I expect some volatility due to the heavy reliance on growth here (with little multiple expansion potential), but this is still looking like a buy here.

TTD Stock Risks

It is possible that the company is unable to compete against the likes of GOOGL, Amazon (AMZN), or simply other DSPs. This might pressure the growth trajectory and the stock might re-rate lower. While the stock looks priced for market-beating returns if it can sustain its aggressive growth rates, it does not look priced with a significant margin of safety if it falls short of that. The near term macro outlook might be choppy as it is still unclear to what extent the tariff escalations have impacted the economy. Investors should be prepared for volatility as the stock is effectively trading at the high end of its fair value range. I can see fair value hovering at the 8x to 12x sales range - the stock trades right around the high end and I emphasize that my projected outperformance needs to come from growth.

TTD Stock Conclusion

TTD is executing well in spite of macro headwinds. The investor base appears to have re-calibrated to more normalized expectations and this might have positive implications for the stock. The company might be able to sustain enviable revenue growth due to the ongoing shift towards digital advertising. I expect the stock to outperform the market due to the aggressive growth rates but caution that multiples are already quite fair here. The mix of a strong secular story, net cash balance sheet, and reasonable valuation are all traits I look for in stocks of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I rate the stock a buy rating.

data.title: Micron: Mispriced Growth Stock Offers Rich Return Prospects
data.description:
MU Remains Attractively Valued Despite The Recent Rally, With Further Upsides Possible

We previously covered Micron Technology (NASDAQ:MU) in April 2025, discussing how the double beat FQ2'25 performance, the robust FQ3'25 guidance, and the promising H2'25 commentary implied that the worst of the memory chip correction was well behind us then.

Despite the elevated inventory levels and the higher net debt position, its improving gross margins signaled financial recovery, aided by the robust data center related spending trends and the discounted valuations.

MU 1Y Stock Price

Since then, MU has already rallied by +42% compared to the wider market at +9%, with a similar stock price recovery also observed in the wider market and the semiconductor sector in varying degrees.

Much of the tailwinds are attributed to the 90-day tariff pause and the ongoing de-escalation in the trade war between China/ the US, significantly aided by the durable AI-related spending trends as reported by numerous semiconductor companies, hyperscalers, AI SaaS companies, and data center REITs during the recent Q1'25 earning season.

The same has been reported by MU, with FQ3'25 revenues of $9.3B (+15.5% QoQ/ +36.5% YoY), richer adj gross margins of 39% (+1.1 points QoQ/ +10.9 YoY/ +46.7 from FY2023 levels of -7.7%/ -7.9 from FY2019 levels of 46.9%), and adj EPS of $1.91 (+22.4% QoQ/ +208% YoY).

Much of the recovery is attributed to the "all-time-high DRAM revenue including nearly 50% sequential growth in HBM revenue," thanks to the more than doubling data-center related revenues and the recovery in the consumer end markets.

These developments are not surprising indeed, since the AI boom requires immense "storage solutions that can handle vast volumes of data, deliver high throughput, offer low-latency access, and store data securely," as both MU and its direct competitor, SK Hynix, qualify their HBM3E offerings with Nvidia (NVDA) and other leading custom AI accelerator designers, while leaving Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) in the dust.

This is on top of MU's ongoing HBM4 sampling for 2026 shipment, the company's tight DRAM/ NAND inventory levels, and the heathier industry-wide inventory levels, with it apparent that the prior 2023/ 2024 memory demand destruction is well behind us indeed, as the management also expects a "demand growth of mid-teens CAGR for both DRAM and NAND" in the medium term, thanks to the recovering demand in the PC, smartphone, and traditional server segments.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why MU has felt confident to offer a rich FQ4'25 revenue guidance of $10.7B (+15% QoQ/ +38% YoY), adj gross margins of 42% (+3 points QoQ/ +5.5 YoY), and adj EPS of $2.50 (+30.8% QoQ/ +111.8% YoY).

These have directly contributed to the consensus' raised forward estimates as well, with the memory chip company expected to report an accelerated top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +15.1%/ +29.7% between the management's FY2025 guidance and FY2027, respectively.

This is compared to the last consensus estimates of +11.6%/ +25.2% and the 5Y historical growth of +8.6%/ +11% (before the memory correction in 2023/ 2024), with it underscoring why the ongoing data center boom has already triggered MU's much needed recovery along with the renewed growth prospects over the next few years.

Thanks to the improving cash flows, the company has also reported healthier balance sheet with a net debt level of -$5.37B (+21% QoQ/ +15.4% YoY/ -355.7% from FY2019 net cash levels of $2.1B), based on the cash/ current debt/ long-term debt calculation method, with things likely to further lift moving forward despite the intensified capex plans to further support the long-term AI demand.

MU Valuations

These reasons are also why MU appears compelling at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 18.14x while nearing the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 18.25x, compared to the sector median of 23.39x and the 5Y mean of 76.27x.

Despite the notable gap compared to its memory chip peer, SSNLF at 12.29x, MU remains reasonably valued at our estimated 2Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.61x as well.

This is based on MU's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 18.14x and the projected 2Y adj EPS growth at a CAGR of +29.7% (based on the management's FY2025 adj EPS guidance of $7.76 and the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $13.07).

When compared to the sector median FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.79x and its semiconductor peers, including:

  • SSNLF at 1.15x,
  • Nvidia (NVDA) at 1.25x,
  • Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) at 1.42x,
  • Broadcom (AVGO) at 1.73x,
  • Marvell (MRVL) at 0.59x, and
  • Qualcomm (QCOM) at 2.20x,

it goes without saying that MU is cheap at current levels, with it offering interested investors with an excellent margin of safety.

So, Is MU Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

MU 5Y Stock Price

For now, the bulls have already defended MU's recent bottom at the $64s ranges along with the +94.6% rally afterwards, as the stock breaks out of numerous resistance levels and the 50/ 100/ 200 day moving averages.

MU's Overbought Levels

Despite MU's apparent overbought levels, historical trends and technical indicators above suggest a potential for further upside before the buying momentum is exhausted, significantly aided by the stable short interest volumes on a YoY basis.

Given the management's FY2025 adj EPS guidance of $7.76 (+496.9% YoY, based on YTD sum of $5.26 and FQ4'25 guidance of $2.50) and the current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 18.14x (cheap compared to its peer group mean of ~25x), it is apparent that the stock is still trading below our fair value estimates of $140.70.

Based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $13.07, there remains an excellent upside potential of +88% to our long-term price target of $237 as well, despite the double digits recovery from the recent April 2025 bottom.

Even so, while we may be reiterating our Buy rating for the MU stock, it comes with the caveat that interested investors observe the stock price movement for a little longer before adding, since its upward momentum is likely to reverse once buying power is exhausted in the intermediate term.

Anyone looking to add may want to do so after a moderate retracement to its prior support levels of $97s and $110s ranges for a lower dollar cost averaging opportunity, with the -17.8% downside from current levels also likely to trigger an improved margin of safety against the rather rich stock market sentiments.

While we remain highly optimistic about MU's long-term prospects during the multi-year data-center related capex boom, some prudence may be warranted in the near-term.

data.title: NCV: Appealing Discount But Weak Track Record To Peers
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

It has been a while since we've given the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund (NCV) a look, going back to early 2023. This is a closed-end fund that combines convertible securities and high-yield bond exposure within the same wrapper. The fund is trading at a historically appealing discount level, and relative to where it was in our prior update, it is also looking like a better value these days. However, the fund has historically been an underperformer relative to its other CEF convertible peers.

They also implemented a reverse 4 to 1 split in February 2025. Even if fundamentally nothing has changed, that still often puts investors in a sour mood. It at least means that there has been considerable NAV erosion over time, and that took the share price down with it. This fund did indeed get to the low single-digit price tag, and that's why the reverse split was done.

NCV Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.47
  • Discount/Premium: -8.84%
  • Distribution Yield: 11.72%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.31%
  • Leverage: 37.32%
  • Managed Assets: $535.90 million
  • Structure: Perpetual

NCV's objective is to "seek total return through a combination of capital appreciation and high current income."

To achieve this, the fund will "invest in a diversified portfolio of domestic convertible securities and high-yield bonds rated below investment grade." They add that "at least 80% of its total assets in a diversified portfolio of convertible securities and non-convertible income-producing securities and seeks to invest at least 50% of its total assets in convertible securities, but determines its allocation based on changes in equity prices, changes in interest rates, and other economic and market factors."

The fund is highly leveraged, and it is primarily through mandatory redeemable preferred shares. These were issued on May 26, 2023, and came with a nearly 6% annualized dividend rate.

These would have been issued to replace the previous cumulative preferred shares issued on September 20, 2018, which came with a dividend rate of 5.625%. In general, they were able to keep the rate from seeing too much of an increase despite a higher rate environment.

When including the borrowing costs, the fund's total expense ratio comes to 3.18% as of the end of fiscal year 2025 reporting.

Performance - Weak Results

First, I'll start with the good news. The fund is trading below its longer-term historical level of the last decade. That's often a sign that the fund could be looking like a relative bargain. At one point, this fund was trading at a premium quite regularly, but it has been a few years now since the fund has seemingly struggled. Basically, since the last distribution cut, it seems to have been under pressure. More on the distribution and the cuts will be discussed below.

Now, for the bad news. The fund is the weakest performer relative to its peer funds in the convertible space. Funds like Calamos Convertible & High Income Fund (CHY), Calamos Convertible Opportunities and Income Fund (CHI) and Advent Convertible & Income Fund (AVK) are the funds that are the most similar, with a heavier allocation to convertibles but then also incorporating high yield corporate bonds.

Ellsworth Growth and Income Fund (ECF) provides convertible exposure as well as an equity sleeve, which can help to explain its outperformance relative to the group. We know equities have done extremely well during this time frame. Bancroft Fund (BCV) is a fund that doesn't mix in too much else outside of convertible securities, so no significant exposure to high-yield corporate bonds like the others.

Still, it doesn't matter; NCV is the worst performer of this group by a rather wide margin. One of the culprits is that the fund has had to deleverage during market drops, which means losses are permanently locked in.

Past performance doesn't indicate future results, but at least in this context, we could see why investors would be choosing to value NCV the way it does if it is a weaker performer. CHY, CHI and AVK, being the closer peers to NCV, are shown as trading at premiums or a shallow discount level. CHY and CHI have actually seen their premiums come down recently from some lofty peaks. That could make them interesting candidates to give a look at this time, in my opinion.

On the other hand, we have ECF and BCV, which trade at the widest discount levels. This is likely because their distribution rates are on the lower end, and they pay quarterly. Though ECF increased its quarterly distribution quite meaningfully recently, and I added to that name recently as well.

Distribution - High Distribution Rate, But Expect Cuts

NCV is one of those funds that put in place a high distribution level and holds it in place for longer than they should. Even now, the distribution rate was cut several years ago, but the annualized distribution rate of 10.68% seems unsustainable. The 10% level is a bit of a red flag for this type of fund, and then you have to factor the operating expenses on top of it—which pushes the required return going forward closer to 14%.

Over the last decade, the fund delivered an annualized total NAV return of 3.33%. This is a driving factor on why the fund has seen its distribution cut many times since its inception, and ultimately, what pushed the fund to that reverse split to have a higher share price.

Remember, a CEF can pay out whatever it'd like until NAV hits $0. However, they will likely never hit zero because they'll implement these reverse splits. Reverse splits, distribution cuts and eroding NAV/share price don't always mean poor total returns—so it is important to realize that as well. That said, for NCV, we already saw that at least historically, for this fund, it has meant poor returns.

When looking at the last annual report, keep in mind that these are pre-reverse split figures. However, all else being equal, the coverage levels wouldn't be impacted in terms of percentages, just the absolute figures. For the reporting twelve months, net investment income coverage would come in at around 36.6%.

That would be an improvement over last year, and with lower rates, we could see that increase. However, due to the primarily fixed-rate preferred leverage they have relative to the rather minimal amount of borrowings based on floating rates, the positive impact could be quite minimal.

For tax purposes, we see that the largest characterization is in the return of capital bucket. Not all ROC is destructive, but in this case, we have seen the NAV declining over time.

NCV's Portfolio

The fund is overall most heavily invested in convertible securities, which is at an allocation we see regularly. In fact, the weighting was quite similar in our prior update when convertibles were at 59.31%—and that was going back to the end of 2022.

In looking at the credit quality breakdown, we naturally see that the fund's highest is listed as "not rated." That isn't that unusual for convertible securities, as they are often issued to qualified institutional buyers. This can help the offerings go through faster, without the time and costs to get the securities rated.

On the other hand, we still have a meaningful sleeve of high-yield corporate bonds, so we see significant exposure to BB and B rated debt securities as well. At the same time, we still have almost 14% in BBB or higher, which is investment-grade.

The combination of these types of securities tends to mean that NCV isn't too interest rate sensitive, and actually would correlate closer to equities. Convertibles often have lower coupons based on the fact that there can be some upside participation if the common stock of the company is performing well. High-yield bonds come with higher relative rates and often with shorter maturities, which helps to keep their duration down.

When investing in a junkier portfolio, one of the approaches is to invest in a high number of securities to minimize the risk of one investment failure tanking the portfolio too heavily. NCV does have that, however, they also have several top holdings that are allocated to the higher quality ratings we saw above. In fact, the top security, being a Wells Fargo (WFC) preferred issue, would be one such example. Even Boeing (BA) just barely makes the mark at a rating of BBB-.

The higher allocations to investment-grade offerings and the overall ~200 holdings help to keep NCV quite diversified.

Conclusion

NCV's valuation in terms of its discount is making it look relatively attractive compared to its historical level. That could provide a potentially better opportunity to invest in this fund. However, it has a rather weak track record relative to its peers. As some of its peers are also looking like they could be providing some potentially better relative valuation levels, they might be more interesting considerations for a long-term investor.

data.title: Macquarie Climate Solutions Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Global markets were mixed in 1Q25, with European markets materially outperforming those in the US. The Trump administration's strategy of "flooding the zone" with numerous economic policies, including frequent tariff threats and abrupt policy changes, created significant trade uncertainty. This approach disrupted established trade relationships and supply chains, leading to anticipated operational challenges for businesses. The constant flux in trade policies made it difficult for companies to plan long-term investments, leading to economic instability.

Additionally, consumer and business sentiment weakened throughout the quarter as investors began to worry about the increasing likelihood of a recession. This apprehension was reflected in the US Federal Reserve (Fed) meeting notes, where interest rates were held steady but indicated at least two additional interest rate cuts in 2025 to mitigate economic slowdown. Inflation remained a persistent concern, exacerbating the overall economic uncertainty.

The quarter also saw significant volatility driven by the debate over excess exuberance for the data center industry and the associated incremental power demand. The rapid expansion of data centers, which require substantial energy resources, created fluctuations in market expectations, further contributing to the volatile economic landscape. Overall, the combination of trade policy disruptions, recession fears, inflation concerns, and sector-specific volatility painted a complex picture for global markets during the quarter. The Fund's benchmark, the MSCI ACWI (All Country World Index) Investable Market Index (IMI), fell 1.61% during the quarter.

Within the Fund's representative sectors of utilities, materials, industrials, and energy - where we typically have the largest exposures and most of our investment opportunities can be found - energy and utilities were the strongest performers in the index overall, with materials and industrials also positive. However, renewable energy companies, including residential solar providers, solar panel manufacturers, and related solar equipment manufacturers have been falling since Donald Trump's inauguration in January in light of policy shifts prioritizing fossil fuel development, cutting renewable subsidies, and halting federal support for solar and wind projects.

The administration has repealed major climate-focused legislation, including provisions within the Inflation Reduction Act, reducing financial incentives for renewable energy expansion. It has also relaxed environmental regulations, further boosting fossil fuel industries at the expense of renewables. Overall, these actions have created uncertainty in the renewable energy sector, driving down investor confidence and stock performance. As such, the WilderHill Clean Energy Index, representative of renewable energy sources such as wind, solar, and other alternative energies, steadily declined over the quarter.

Within the Fund

For 1Q25, Macquarie Climate Solutions Fund (MUTF:IVEIX) Institutional Class shares slightly underperformed the Fund's benchmark, the MSCI ACWI IMI.

Despite the changed outlook post the Trump election in which clean energy stocks experienced, and continue to experience, steep declines as discussed above, the Fund is only slightly underperforming its broad-based index. This is largely due to the Fund's heavy overweight to the utilities sector, the second strongest-performing sector within the index for the quarter. RWE AG was the strongest-performing utility stock within the Fund for the quarter. This sector overweight is a key part of the Fund's strategy, as we believe it to be an area where meaningful opportunities can be found in closing the emissions gap. We also see the sector's defensiveness as a boon for portfolio diversification and downside protection, relative to other climate-focused funds.

While energy was the strongest-performing sector within the index for the quarter, stock selection within the Fund in energy was a key detractor. This was primarily driven by poor performance from Neste Oyj. (OTCPK:NTOIF) Neste has a focus on renewable products, including renewable diesel, renewable jet fuels and solutions, renewable solvents, as well as raw materials for bioplastics. Given the unfavorable environment for renewable stocks discussed above, Neste's stock suffered.

Information technology and consumer discretionary were the two weakest-performing sectors in the index for the quarter. Within IT, exposure to solar within the Fund was a key detractor from performance, led by First Solar Inc. (FSLR) Despite having its production contracted through the end of the decade, concerns about reduced spending on renewables and the potential reversal of incentives from the Inflation Reduction Act by the Trump administration weighed on performance. We continue to hold First Solar, however, as we believe it may be well positioned given its build-out of utility-scale solar capabilities.

SunRun Inc. (RUN), within the industrials sector, one of the four representative sectors for the Fund, was also significantly down. As of 2Q25, we have fully exited SunRun. Within consumer discretionary, Amazon.com Inc. (AMZN) is the Fund's only holding, and it underperformed an already poorly performing sector for the quarter. With announcements of high capital expenditures (more than $100 billion in 2025) towards key themes including artificial intelligence infrastructure and data centers, concerns about near-term profitability arose despite long-term growth potential. Our conviction in the long-term viability of the company remains, given its exposure to these themes, both from a financial and sustainability perspective. As the top buyer of clean energy by far in 2024 (according to BloombergNEF), the company remains on track to be an impactful reducer of its greenhouse gas emissions.

NET Power Inc. (NPWR), a clean energy technology company focused on its proprietary NET Power Cycle for transforming natural gas into clean power, was the largest detractor for the Fund over the quarter. The company experienced a significant earnings miss, project delays, and cost overruns related to its Project Permian. We exited the position during the quarter following significant negative returns.

Waste Management Inc. (WM) was the top contributor to the Fund's performance for the quarter. Within the industrials sector, the company has diverse operations in collection and disposal, recycling, renewable energy, and healthcare solutions. Its positive returns were driven by several factors including its sustainability initiatives. The company's investments in renewable natural gas projects and recycling technologies improved operational efficiency and aligned with global environmental trends enhancing long-term growth prospects, despite the aforementioned headwinds for renewables within the US.

Recent climate solutions news

February 2025: "Amazon again tops global clean power buyers with 102 deals"Amazon, a "reducer" in the Fund helping to decarbonize industry, was the top buyer of clean energy last year, purchasing more solar and wind power than the next two companies in the ranking, Google and Meta, combined. Corporations are continuing to ramp up their clean power purchases and 2024 was another record year, with 62.2 gigawatts of deals tracked by BloombergNEF. Amazon announced 102 power purchase agreements totaling 10.3 gigawatts across 12 markets and accounted for 16% of global volume.

Source: Bloomberg.

March 2025: "Schneider Electric Unveils the Future of Energy Intelligence with One Digital Grid Platform" Schneider Electric SE, a "facilitator" in the Fund and the global leader in the digital transformation of energy management and automation, is revolutionizing grid operations with its launch of the One Digital Grid Platform, an integrated and Al-powered platform designed to enhance grid resiliency, reliability and efficiency. This platform provides the data and technical foundation to integrate independent software solutions, enabling utilities to accelerate grid modernization and deliver cleaner, more affordable energy while benefitting from lower total cost of ownership. This is a prime example of the synergies between reducers, inclusive of utilities, and facilitators within our Fund.

Available in 2025, the One Digital Grid Platform equips utilities with the foundation needed to navigate modern energy challenges. The platform offers a complete ecosystem of solutions, leveraging AI to deliver generational advances in planning & asset management, operations & resiliency, and flexibility & customer engagement. Schneider Electric joins the Electric Power Research Institute (EPRI) in a global effort to solve data center grid-capacity constraints.

Source: PRNewsWire.

Outlook

Looking forward, we continue to have conviction that there is still some positivity to expect for climate solutions stocks under the Trump administration, as well as internationally. While we expect that a portion of our Fund may continue to be negatively impacted by the political and economic environment, namely regarding negative incentives and resulting sentiment around renewables, as well as the imposition of a high level of tariffs resulting in what may be the biggest trade shock in US history, we have structured our Fund so as to ideally be more diversified and defensive than the traditional clean energy or climate change focused Fund in the market. That is, while renewables like solar get caught up in the US repeal of incentives as well as the trade war, one of our focuses includes the utilities sector, which as discussed above is a strong overweight. We believe this positions us well given utilities' defensiveness, as proven over the quarter.

Further, while growth in renewable energy may be volatile over the near term within the US, renewable power sources globally including solar, wind, hydroelectric, geothermal and biomass can help to meet the world's growing energy needs, including the impending growth spurred by AI data centers. As noted in the prior quarter, renewable power is now the cheapest and quickest source of power to deploy, and companies have an increasing desire for clean power to satisfy their sustainability goals and stakeholder demands.

Macquarie Climate Solutions Fund

Specifically, despite the significant pullback in solar equities on the removal of government incentives by the Trump administration, we remain bullish on the sector. As noted above, we continue to hold a position in First Solar. Although the near-term outlook is uncertain, we believe the company's robust domestic manufacturing base and solid balance sheet position it well to navigate this volatility. Looking ahead, the outlook for solar deployment remains promising based on increasing global demand for clean energy and advancements in solar technology.

Lastly, institutional investors are important contributors. These asset owners have been significantly increasing their commitments to climate solutions, for example the New York City Employees' Retirement System (NYCERS) exceeded its interim 2025 goal with a total $4.47 billion in commitments, and CalPERS has announced a $100 b Climate Action Plan. We expect this trend to persist and as such, we believe our investable universe of reducers and facilitators will benefit.

In April 2025, Macquarie Group Limited and Nomura Holding America Inc. (Nomura) announced that they had entered into an agreement for Nomura to acquire Macquarie Asset Management's US and European public i1nvestments business. The transaction is subject to customary closing conditions, including the receipt of applicable regulatory, client, and shareholder approvals. Subject to such approvals and the satisfaction of these conditions, the transaction is expected to close by the end of 2025.

data.title: Macquarie High Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

US high yield bonds, as measured by the ICE BofA US Cash Pay High Yield Index, returned 0.97% for 1Q25, while European and global high yield bonds returned 0.66% and 1.25%, as measured by the ICE BofA Euro High Yield Index and the ICE BofA US Dollar Global High Yield Index, respectively.

The ICE BofA US Cash Pay High Yield Index returned 1.39% in January as a positive risk tone across markets drove spreads toward historical lows. The rally was underpinned by a combination of positive macroeconomic data, a solid start to 4Q earnings, and favorable technicals that followed weaker performance in December. The market was also supported by low default activity. CCC-rated bonds were the strongest performers, returning 1.70% for the month, followed by B-rated and BB-rated securities, which returned 1.42% and 1.29%, respectively.

Volatility picked up meaningfully in February as weaker economic data and tariff uncertainty brought growth concerns to the foreground. Consequently, spreads widened 18 basis points, with notable decompression by rating category (one basis point is a hundredth of a percentage point). Still, total returns remained positive as wider spreads were more than offset by lower Treasury yields. The ICE BofA US Cash Pay High Yield Index returned 0.67% for the month with bonds rated BB,B, and CCC returning 0.72%,0.60%, and 0.61%, respectively. Fundamentally, 4 Q earnings generally exceeded expectations, though we noted sector-level dispersion and forward guidance from management teams was more mixed.

In March, the risk-off tone worsened as weaker growth data and persistently elevated core inflation readings stoked stagflation concerns while policy uncertainty remained high. The ICE BofA US Cash Pay High Yield Index declined 1.07% as spreads widened 67 basis points on average while Treasury yields were volatile, ending the month only modestly lower. Unsurprisingly, rating decompression accelerated during this period with bonds rated CCC, B, and BB declining 2.81%,1.31%, and 0.55%, respectively, as higher-quality risk meaningfully outperformed.

For the quarter, returns remained positive for bonds rated BB and B , returning 1.43% and 0.77% respectively, while bonds rated CCC lagged and fell 0.89%. The leading sectors were food & beverage, which gained 2.06%; financials, up 1.89%; and cable/satellite, up 1.77%. The weakest sectors were diversified media, which declined 0.90%; paper/packaging, down 0.69%; and automotive, up 0.03%.

New-issue volume totaled $68.3 billion for the quarter, down 22% year over year. Refinancings comprised 75% of this activity, followed by mergers and acquisitions (M&A) at 15.3%, and general corporate purposes at 6.6%. The 12month par-weighted US high yield default rate ended the quarter at 1.20%, and the outlook for the 2025 default rate stood at approximately 2%-3%.

Within the Fund

For 1Q25, Macquarie High Income Fund (MUTF:IVHIX) Institutional Class shares underperformed the Fund's benchmark, the ICE BofA US High Yield Constrained Index.

Key contributors to performance included:

  • An overweight to and security selection within the media sector
  • Security selection within the technology sector
  • Security selection within the utility sector
  • An overweight to the banking sector
  • An overweight to the insurance sector

Key detractors from performance included:

  • Security selection within the leisure sector
  • Security selection within the basic industry sector
  • Security selection within the telecommunications sector
  • Security selection within the consumer goods sector
  • An underweight to and security selection within the real estate sector

Outlook

Following the recent uptick in volatility, we expect sustained policy and macroeconomic uncertainty to keep spreads somewhat elevated from recent lows. Corporate fundamentals entered this period from a position of relative strength, but forward guidance from our management teams suggests that we should expect more sector level dispersion with cyclical and tariff-impacted credits most exposed at this time. Still, solid corporate balance sheets, low default-rate expectations around 2%−3%, and attractive all-in yields for the asset class at 7.66% should help contain upside risk to spreads outside of a recession scenario. As always, we will continue to rely on our disciplined, research-driven investment process to capitalize on opportunities when valuations diverge from fundamentals.

Sources: Bloomberg and J.P. Morgan, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: H&K AG: The NATO Premium Is More Than Priced In
data.description:
Introduction

H&K AG (OTC:MLHKF/MLHK.PA) is a German firearms manufacturer with a long history in the defense space. If you’ve ever glanced at a NATO equipment list, you’ve seen their work — HK416, MP5, G36. They’re in widespread use across militaries, special forces, and law enforcement. With global defense budgets climbing, especially in Europe, the setup has been favorable. In 2024, they posted a solid year: revenue up 13.9%, net income up 40.4%, backlog strong heading into 2025. Business-wise, things look fine.

But the stock is priced like something it’s not. It trades at 122x trailing earnings, over 110x forward, 10x sales, and 56x EBITDA. For a mid-sized gun manufacturer, those numbers just don’t make sense. There’s no transformative step-change happening here — no huge IP moat, no scalable software margin. What you’ve got is a well-run, capital-intensive business doing what it’s supposed to — but that’s not nearly enough to justify this kind of multiple. I’m rating it a Strong Sell.

Defense Niche, Global Reach, and Procurement Tailwinds

The business is pretty focused, which is actually part of its strength. They aren’t chasing every adjacent category — it’s rifles, pistols, submachine guns, and related weapon systems. That’s what they do. In 2024, assault rifles made up 35% of revenue, pistols 22%, machine/submachine guns 21%, and the rest — about 22% — came from what they call “related systems,” which probably includes mounting platforms, optics, and support gear. The customer mix is still defense-heavy: 62% military, 6% police and other public sector, and 32% civilian. So it’s not just a government supplier, but close.

Geographically, they’re concentrated in NATO-aligned countries — which is intentional. FY2024 breakdown: U.S. 28%, Germany 20%, France 12%, U.K. 9%, and the rest split across vetted export markets. The “Green Country Strategy” limits who they sell to — no gray-market governments, only NATO, EU, Switzerland, Australia, Japan, and a few others Germany pre-approves. That restricts the total addressable market, sure, but it helps avoid licensing headaches and reputational risk. And it makes order visibility a little better.

Macro environment still favors defense spend. NATO’s 2% GDP guideline is now politically locked in across most of Europe. Germany’s recent constitutional change (March 2025) specifically carves out more fiscal headroom for defense — which, if followed through, will likely benefit H&K over the next few years. Doesn’t mean immediate orders, but it sets the stage.

They’ve also begun supporting Ukrainian defense needs — including rifles adapted for Soviet-era calibers. Those programs should contribute to 2025 revenue. On the product side, they’re putting money into modularity and fire control systems — pretty much table stakes in modern procurement cycles, but necessary. It’s not revolutionary tech, but it keeps them in the conversation.

Lastly, they acquired Chrom-Müller in late 2024 — a chrome-plating shop. Not flashy, but it brings an essential process in-house, improves control over finish quality, and should reduce bottlenecks. Also helpful for preserving margin, particularly with materials inflation still hanging around.

2024 & Q1 2025: Revenue Climb, Orderbook Records, but Margins Plateau

Financially, 2024 was solid. Revenue rose 13.9% to €343.4 million. Net income increased 40.4% to €26.98 million. EPS went from €0.54 to €0.76. EBITDA edged up to €63.1 million, EBIT slipped slightly to €49.5 million. Gross profit declined modestly to €114.1 million — higher-cost mix was the culprit — but management still called it their second-best profit year on record. Backlog hit €289.3 million, and they booked €426.2 million in new orders — an all-time high for the company.

Metric FY2024 FY2023 YoY Change
Revenue €343.4 million €301.4 million +13.9%
Gross Profit €114.1 million €115.9 million -1.6%
EBIT €49.5 million €50.4 million -1.8%
EBITDA €63.1 million €62.2 million +1.4%
Net Income €26.98 million €19.2 million +40.4%
EPS (basic) €0.76 €0.54 +40.7%
Backlog €289.3 million €210.0 million +37.8%
New Orders €426.2 million €285.3 million +49.4%

Q1 2025 picked up where last year left off. Revenue was up 8.8% YoY to €81.6 million. Gross profit rose €4.9 million to €27.1 million. EBITDA jumped 59.8% to €14.7 million, with some mix improvement and scale benefits. Operating profit hit €10.7 million. Net income more than doubled to €5.7 million, EPS landing at €0.16 vs €0.07 last year. There was a €2.1 million FX loss, but a €1.2 million derivative gain offset part of it.

Metric Q1 2025 Q1 2024 YoY Change
Revenue €81.6 million €75.0 million +8.8%
Gross Profit €27.1 million €22.2 million +22.1%
EBIT €10.7 million €6.1 million +75.4%
EBITDA €14.7 million €9.2 million +59.8%
Net Income €5.7 million €2.4 million +137.5%
EPS (basic) €0.16 €0.07 +128.6%

Management expects a “mid-double-digit million” euro bump in 2025 revenue — likely €30–40 million — and a slight increase in EBITDA. That implies maybe 5–7% operating profit growth. If you take trailing net income of €30.3 million and give them the benefit of that 5–8% lift, you get to €31.8–32.7 million in earnings. Which means you’re still paying ~114–117x earnings for what is essentially a modestly growing industrial. The problem isn’t that it’s shrinking — it’s that this kind of growth just doesn’t match the multiple.

Working Capital Burn, Solid Liquidity, and Debt Paydown

Cash generation was strong last year. €50.4 million from operations brought the cash pile to €69.5 million at year-end. But by Q1 2025, cash had dropped to €44.3 million. Most of that was working capital related — €21.5 million in contract liabilities unwound as prepayments turned into shipments, and inventories climbed €13.6 million ahead of bigger production runs.

Operating cash flow was negative €15.7 million for the quarter. Capex was manageable at €2.3 million — €1.1 million on R&D, €1.6 million on machinery. They repaid €5 million on Facility A (bringing the total to €15 million since early 2024), and interest payments ran €1.6 million. Another €0.4 million went toward lease obligations.

Liquidity’s still fine. They ended Q1 with €44.3 million in cash and had €50 million in credit availability, with €46 million undrawn. Equity ticked up to €138.6 million from €133.4 million. No near-term maturities. No immediate concerns here — it’s a pretty clean balance sheet.

Valuation Disconnected: Premium Multiples, Low-Margin Reality

This is where the numbers start to feel surreal. The stock trades at 10.64x sales, 122.4x earnings, and 56.2x EV/EBITDA, compared to sector medians of around 1.5x sales, 24x P/E, and 13x EV/EBITDA. So you’re paying four to eight times the industry norm for a business that’s competent, steady, and unremarkable in its fundamentals.

Company P/S P/E EV/EBITDA Revenue Growth (YoY) Net Income Growth (YoY)
H&K AG 10.64 122.35 56.15 +13.9% +40.4%
Colt CZ Group SE (OTCPK:CZGZF/CZG.PR) 1.56 30.96 11.09 +50.6% -48.9%
Smith & Wesson Brands, Inc. (SWBI) 0.81 28.63 8.44 +11.8% +7.4%
Sturm, Ruger & Company, Inc. (RGR) 1.14 19.43 7.98 -1.5% -36.6%
Taurus Armas S.A. (TASA3.SA) 0.63 13.09 7.99 -6.1% -49.9%
Howa Machinery, Ltd. (6203.T) 0.51 16.24 10.21 +25.5% N/A (Turned Profitable)

Take Colt CZ. Similar scale, same customers. Colt grew revenue over 50% last year (albeit with earnings volatility), and trades at 1.56x P/S, 30.96x P/E, 11.09x EV/EBITDA. That’s still high, but nowhere near this.

Smith & Wesson and Ruger? Not perfect comps — more U.S. civilian exposure — but they’re real manufacturers with consistent output. Smith trades at 0.81x sales, 28.63x P/E, 8.44x EV/EBITDA. Ruger is under 8x EV/EBITDA despite some earnings contraction.

Even smaller names like Taurus Armas (0.63x P/S, 13.09x P/E) and Howa Machinery (0.51x P/S, 10.21x EV/EBITDA) show what the rest of the industry gets valued at. These aren’t subpar companies — they’re just treated like normal manufacturers.

H&K, meanwhile, is being priced like a national champion or strategic asset. That’s the real issue. The multiple has less to do with financials and more to do with optics — NATO relevance, brand recognition, defense tailwinds. But this is still a factory business. You don’t get SaaS-level valuations for delivering rifles, no matter how accurate they are.

Catalysts: Bundeswehr Deliveries, Exports, and Operational Tweaks

The clearest driver is the Bundeswehr assault rifle contract — up to 118,718 HK416 A8 units under a €209 million award. Around €25 million shipped in 2024, but the bulk of it ramps starting late 2025 at 20,000 rifles per year through 2030. That gives a floor to revenue, and some margin contribution, though nothing transformational.

They also secured a 500-unit order for the G210 sniper rifle. Not huge, but it might open the door to larger framework deals. Police and special forces orders are still in the picture too.

On exports, H&K’s new Eastern-caliber platforms could benefit from continued Ukraine aid — if donor countries keep funding gear that works with legacy stockpiles, there’s runway there. And U.S./EU law enforcement demand, while not booming, is steady.

Internally, they’re investing in modularity and fire control — no one’s buying without it anymore — and the Chrom-Müller deal should help stabilize margins as volume scales. Small stuff, but in aggregate it matters.

Risks: Valuation Complacency, Government Dependence, and Limited Float

The biggest risk is valuation itself. At 122x earnings, you don’t need a bad quarter — a soft quarter is enough. Any delay in backlog conversion, any miss on mix or margin, and you’re looking at 30–50% downside just to re-rate to peer levels.

Customer concentration is another real issue. A large portion of revenue still depends on the German government. And while the constitutional changes are a tailwind, procurement remains political. Delays, legal wrangling, and administrative resets aren’t uncommon.

Input costs are another lever. Not all contracts allow price escalation, and inflation hasn’t fully cooled. Wages, materials, components — all still climbing. Plus, FX losses are showing up — €2.1 million in Q1 — and while derivatives help, they don’t eliminate the risk.

Also worth noting: the stock barely trades. Just 683 shares per day, on average. That limits institutional interest, makes entering/exiting harder, and adds slippage. There’s no near-term liquidity crunch, but hybrid capital and slow-amortizing loans tie up some optionality.

Conclusion

H&K is doing its job. It’s not flailing. It has a long-standing presence in the NATO small arms market, a clean balance sheet, and steady defense demand. The Bundeswehr rifle program gives years of visibility, and the Ukraine-facing platforms show they’re still evolving.

But 114–117x forward earnings? That’s just not tenable. It’s a fine company, but it’s being priced like a strategic growth asset in a business where growth is measured in percentages, not multiples. This is not a software firm. It’s a manufacturing company with real costs and real constraints. I rate H&K AG a Strong Sell.

data.title: AMG Frontier Small Cap Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The AMG Frontier Small Cap Growth Fund (MUTF:MSSVX) (Class N ) returned −14.43% for the first quarter of 2025, compared to the −11.12% return for its benchmark, the Russell 2000 Growth Index. For the 12 months ending March 31, 2025, the Fund returned −14.50%, which lagged behind the benchmark return of −4.86%. Please note that this Fund has multiple share classes.

U.S. equities moved lower in the first quarter, chiefly during the last several weeks of the period. The market decline was spurred by investor fears of lower growth due to the economic policies of the new administration, particularly those related to trade. Small caps trailed large caps as the Russell 2000® Index declined 9.5% while the Russell 1000® Index only declined 4.5%. Growth stocks underperformed as the Russell 2000® Growth declined 11.1%.

Our largest detracting industry was technology, with roughly half of the underperformance attributed to our overweight position and the other half from negative stock selection.

indie Semiconductor Inc. (INDI), a designer of semiconductors for both internal combustion engine powered cars and electric vehicles, was the worst performer. While the company guided first quarter revenue up year-over-year, the guidance was slightly below street estimates due to weakness in China. The stock continued to sell off as tariff uncertainty grew, calling into question auto build rates worldwide. indie has excellent growth prospects driven by its strong design-win momentum in many geographies. Additionally, given the company's desirable portfolio of products, it remains a potential acquisition candidate if an industry slowdown persists.

Another underperformer was Ultra Clean Holdings, Inc. (UCTT), a manufacturer of critical subsystems and services for semiconductor capital equipment manufacturers and semi fabs. While Ultra Clean's December quarter results exceeded consensus expectations, its outlook for the March quarter disappointed due to product qualification delays at a top Chinese customer and weakening semiconductor equipment demand in China. Management expects the qualification delays to be resolved soon and still anticipates overall company sales to outpace semiconductor wafer fabrication equipment (WFE) industry spending in 2025. Longer term, Ultra Clean is well positioned to gain market share through new design wins at top-tier semi cap equipment manufacturers.

Industrials was the second main area of underperformance, mostly due to stock selection. Our worst industrial name was BILL Holdings, Inc. (BILL), a payments company that services small businesses. When BILL reported its fourth quarter financial results, investors became concerned about the company's ability to maintain its margins due to lower adoption rates of its more profitable payment service offerings. Additionally, during the period, market sentiment towards small businesses turned markedly negative. We reduced our position in BILL as it appears the ongoing consumer confidence weakness will continue to hamper small businesses spending.

A second industrial underperformer was FTAI Aviation Ltd (FTAI), which has been one of our top contributors over the past couple of years. In January, a hedge fund published a short report asserting that FTAI had inflated its earnings through dubious accounting practices. The stock plummeted on the news, but we felt confident those claims were without merit. This event triggered the company to hire an independent auditor to review its accounting practices. During this time, we added to the position at lower prices where it presented a very attractive risk/reward given our confidence in the company's business model, growth opportunities, and understanding of its accounting practices. As it stands today, the investigation by the independent auditors has been completed with findings of no wrongdoing by FTAI, no accounting restatements, no delay in any Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) filings, and no changes to earnings projections. We anticipate many years of strong growth ahead and have maintained a significant portfolio position.

Our best contributing stock was Tempus AI (TEM), gaining 43% on the back of robust growth and the end of its initial public offering lockup period. Tempus is a health care company that combines its next generation diagnostics platform across multiple diseases with its leading data and analytics capabilities. Tempus is rooted in clinical care delivery as one of the largest sequencers of cancer patients and patients with other diseases. The company is building an intelligent diagnostics platform through the application of its artificial intelligence capabilities to allow physicians to deliver more personalized and data-driven therapies to patients and to help researchers develop more precise therapies. The Tempus platform collects numerous data types, and unlike peers, the data comes not only from its own labs but also from a variety of sources including more than 2,000 health care institutions and more than half of the practicing U.S. oncologists. Today, Tempus has a multimodal, de-identified database that is much larger than any other company or institution. This database creates a substantial economic moat and additional revenue stream for the company when it licenses the data to pharma companies to aid in therapy development. The company's recent rapid growth has been driven both by increased testing volumes, higher realized average selling prices, as more payors are covering their tests, and improved coverage and price through Medicare. While not yet profitable, Tempus guided slightly positive Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization (EBITDA) for 2025, a meaningful improvement compared to 2024 and prior periods.

The market volatility has created several opportunities to invest in companies at prices we find attractive, and thus we have initiated new positions in a variety of industries. Examples include CACI International Inc (CACI), Amentum Holdings, Inc. (AMTM), Essential Properties Realty Trust, Inc. (EPRT), and DigitalOcean Holdings, Inc. (DOCN) CACI and Amentum both provide essential services to the Department of Defense and other governmental agencies. These companies recently saw a dramatic decline in their stock prices as investors became concerned that the newly formed Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) would recommend cuts across all government areas. While pruning inefficient spending is likely, we believe that the mission critical nature of the projects on which CACI and Amentum work, together with the considerable cost the government would incur to bring this work in-house, are testaments to the value these companies provide. While some projects may be delayed, increased spending on defense and digital infrastructure should enable these companies to grow earnings during the next few years.

Essential Properties owns and leases properties with a focus on regional and local mid-level markets. It is well capitalized and has a low percentage of leases rolling off over the next few years. Essential Properties is generating sector leading investment spreads, which should provide it with opportunities to grow faster than peers, boost its earnings, and potentially increase its multiple as well. DigitalOcean provides cloud computing infrastructure to developers and small businesses. DigitalOcean's new CEO has increased investment in its service offering, which should enable the company to grow its share of wallet and reduce churn in the customer base. As use cases for Al develop, we expect more developers will use DigitalOcean's services to build their businesses in a more cost-effective manner, driving increasing earnings for years to come.

As we enter the second quarter, market volatility has increased due to the administration's changing trade policies. In our fourth quarter letter we wrote:

The prospect of tariffs is front and center once again and it is unclear to what extent these will be implemented. Potential changes to immigration, tax rates and government cuts through the new Department of Government Efficiency could each have a bearing on the economy, while a renewed focus on U.S. manufacturing could affect the administration's attitude towards the U.S. Dollar. It seems to us that each of these initiatives will influence various parts of the economy in different ways and will likely lead to periods of turbulence as many sectors could move in and out of favor rapidly.

Indeed, we witnessed this sentiment play out with various segments of the market performing well for several weeks, but then crashing as the administration's actions around tariffs quickly escalated. Ironically, many of the companies whose stocks were hurt most are poised to benefit greatly from some of the initiatives this administration is attempting to implement. We believe the primary challenge at present is that amid a multi-layered policy shift, the spotlight is currently on only one layer: global trade. Ideally, as the next several weeks and months elapse, we should slowly gain clarity on the tariff impact, realize the benefits of lower deficits in the next governmental budget, understand the administration's tax bill, and start to look forward to the advantages of onshoring and capital investment in the United States. However, in the near term, investors remain concentrated on the vast and unpredictable impact of tariffs and the possibility that can cause a recession. With these concerns in mind, we are focused on analyzing the current and future competitive positions of our holdings, combined with the balance sheet risks and geographical exposures of the businesses.

We thank you again for investing with us. We hope you will call with any questions.
